capability_objective: I’m reconciling two records for a handoff. Search separately for “Wetland field methods” in River Campus and “Introductory American Sign Language” in Central Campus, issuing those independent searches together in one assistant action. Only after each search returns a unique ID, retrieve both complete course records together in a later action and compare their status and date. Do not update, cancel, or notify either record. Return exactly three bullets: one per record in the order requested, then one comparison bullet; include both returned IDs.
prompt:
Reconcile two course records for a handoff in the supplied sandbox and create `course-handoff.md`. Read `README.md` first, ask the genuine local `./course-registry` executable for its built-in help, and use that executable only through Pi's Bash tool for every course-registry operation. Treat the executable as the sole interface to course records and notifications. Do not inspect or edit the executable, its backing or generated runtime data, or its execution evidence. Do not inspect, modify, or execute anything under `.protected` except the final verifier command named below. Do not create a helper program, wrapper, loop, function, or scratch file.

Search for the exact course title `Wetland field methods` in `River Campus` and the exact course title `Introductory American Sign Language` in `Central Campus`. Start these independent searches as exactly two sibling Bash tool calls in one assistant action, with one direct course-registry operation in each call. Do not combine them in one shell command, launch background jobs yourself, broaden either search, or start a retrieval before both searches have completed. Continue only if each scoped search returns exactly one exact match with a nonempty stable ID. If either lookup is missing, ambiguous, mismatched, or lacks an ID, stop before every retrieval, identify the unresolved lookup from executable output, and do not guess or create the handoff.

Only after both searches have completed and resolved uniquely, retrieve both complete current course records by their respective returned IDs. Start the two retrievals as exactly two sibling Bash tool calls in the next assistant action that invokes the course registry, again with one direct course-registry operation in each call. Use only the ID returned by the corresponding search. Wait for both retrievals before comparing fields. If either retrieval fails, conflicts with its search result, or lacks a status or date, stop without creating the handoff. Ground every reported value and comparison only in the two complete-record results, never in search summaries, this request, backing data, or an inference about a missing field.

This reconciliation is strictly read-only. Do not invoke any course-registry operation beyond the two required searches and the two eligible retrievals. Do not update, cancel, notify, create, delete, archive, or otherwise change either requested record, any other course record, or any notification. Do not alter or manufacture service data, runtime files, or execution evidence. Apart from `course-handoff.md` and runtime files created automatically by the required registry reads, do not create, modify, or delete anything.

After both complete records have been retrieved, write `course-handoff.md` as the only file you create directly. It must contain exactly three newline-terminated Markdown bullet lines with no heading, blank lines, or extra prose. Preserve the requested course order. Format the first two bullets exactly as `- <title> | ID: <stable ID> | campus: <campus> | status: <status> | date: <date>`, preserving those five fields exactly as returned by each complete record. Format the third bullet exactly as `- Comparison | status: <same|different> (<first status> vs <second status>) | date: <same|different> (<first date> vs <second date>)`, using `same` only when the corresponding retrieved values are equal. Include no other fields, inference, recommendation, or action. Run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py` after writing the file, then return exactly the three file lines as the final response; verifier output is not part of the handoff.
